**Lucio Colletti** (Italian: [[ˈluːtʃo kolˈletti]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 8 December 1924 – 3 November 2001) was an Italian [Western Marxist](/wiki/Western_Marxist "Western Marxist") philosopher. Colletti started to be known outside Italy because of a long interview with him that [Marxist historian](/wiki/Marxist_historian "Marxist historian") [Perry Anderson](/wiki/Perry_Anderson "Perry Anderson") published in the *[New Left Review](/wiki/New_Left_Review "New Left Review")* in 1974.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucio_Colletti&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Colletti studied philosophy at the [Sapienza University of Rome](/wiki/Sapienza_University_of_Rome "Sapienza University of Rome"), where he earned a *[laurea](/wiki/Laurea "Laurea")* with a thesis entitled *La logica di Benedetto Croce* (*The Logic of [Benedetto Croce](/wiki/Benedetto_Croce "Benedetto Croce")*), which was supervised by [Carlo Antoni](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Antoni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carlo Antoni (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlo_Antoni "it:Carlo Antoni")].[[2]](#cite_note-2) Inspired by the [Western Marxist](/wiki/Western_Marxist "Western Marxist") philosopher [Galvano Della Volpe](/wiki/Galvano_Della_Volpe "Galvano Della Volpe"), he then gravitated towards [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Colletti was well known as a critic of [Hegelian idealism](/wiki/Hegelian_idealism "Hegelian idealism") and later became a noted critic of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"). He wrote the foreword for the Italian edition of [Alfred Schmidt](/wiki/Alfred_Schmidt_(philosopher) "Alfred Schmidt (philosopher)")'s *[The Concept of Nature in Marx](/wiki/The_Concept_of_Nature_in_Marx "The Concept of Nature in Marx")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Colletti changed his political beliefs very often and abandoned many of his early Marxist ideals. Having been a member of the anti-fascist [Action Party](/wiki/Action_Party_(Italy) "Action Party (Italy)") (*Partito d'Azione*; PdA) in his youth, he joined the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) in 1949 and emerged as an important cultural party figure.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1964, Coletti left the PCI because the party's break with its semi-[Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinist "Stalinist") past was leading towards what he called, in his view, a "patently rightward direction."[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the 1970s he was among the supporters of Socialist leader [Bettino Craxi](/wiki/Bettino_Craxi "Bettino Craxi"). From 1996 until his death he was elected on the list of [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(1994) "Forza Italia (1994)"), [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi")'s [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") political party, as a member of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") (lower house) in the Italian parliament.

## Selected publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lucio_Colletti&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Selected publications")]

* [The Manifesto of 101](/wiki/The_Manifesto_of_101 "The Manifesto of 101")
* "The Theory of the Crash". *Telos*, 13 (Fall 1972). New York: Telos Press.
* 1972 (1974) *From Rousseau to Lenin*
* 1973 (1979) *Marxism and Hegel*
